steps.title: Read Text 2's myth claim
steps.content: Text 1 treats the audience's panic at Arrival of the Train as a real turning point that shows film transforming culture. Text 2 says there is no contemporary evidence that the first audience was alarmed, even though the story has become canonical. It argues that people keep retelling the story because they already believe film's arrival was transformative, so the story works as a founding myth separating a naive before from a sophisticated after.

steps.title: Separate myth from evidence
steps.content: Choice B says film historians regard the transformative view as naive, but Text 2 says the story is canonical even among them. Choice C adds a claim that historians see themselves as more sophisticated than modern audiences, which Text 2 does not say; the naive and sophisticated labels describe the myth's before-and-after structure. Choice D says the misconception captures early audiences' real alarm, contradicting Text 2's point that there is no contemporary evidence for that alarm. Choice A matches Text 2's view that the description is driven more by belief in film's significance than by facts. The answer is A.
